title: Ash Wednesday

infobox.value: [Holy Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)"), [Divine Service](/wiki/Divine_Service_(Lutheran) "Divine Service (Lutheran)"), [Holy Qurbana](/wiki/Holy_Qurbana "Holy Qurbana"), [Service of worship](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") [Fasting](/wiki/Fasting#Christianity "Fasting") and [abstinence](/wiki/Abstinence#Abstinence_in_religion "Abstinence") Placing of ashes on the head

is a holy day of [prayer](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") and [fasting](/wiki/Christian_fasting "Christian fasting") in many [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christian "Western Christian") denominations. It is preceded by [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday") and marks the first day of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"): the seven weeks of prayer, fasting and [almsgiving](/wiki/Alms#Christianity "Alms") before the arrival of [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Cumming2025-2)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

First day of Lent in Western Christianity

This article is about the holy day in Christianity. For other uses, see [Ash Wednesday (disambiguation)](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday_(disambiguation) "Ash Wednesday (disambiguation)").

| Ash Wednesday | |

| Date | 46 days before [Easter Sunday](/wiki/Easter_Sunday "Easter Sunday") |
| 2025 date | 5 March |
| 2026 date | 18 February |
| 2027 date | 10 February |
| 2028 date | 1 March |
| Duration | 24 hours |

**Ash Wednesday** is a holy day of [prayer](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") and [fasting](/wiki/Christian_fasting "Christian fasting") in many [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christian "Western Christian") denominations. It is preceded by [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday") and marks the first day of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"): the seven weeks of prayer, fasting and [almsgiving](/wiki/Alms#Christianity "Alms") before the arrival of [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Cumming2025-2)

Ash Wednesday is observed by [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") of the [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), [Moravian](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") ([Episcopalian](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_(United_States) "Episcopal Church (United States)")), and [United Protestant](/wiki/United_Protestant "United Protestant") denominations, as well as by some churches in the [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") (including certain [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist"), [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), and [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") churches), [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist") and [Nazarene](/wiki/Church_of_the_Nazarene "Church of the Nazarene") traditions.[[3]](#cite_note-2018RCA-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Lipin2017-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Methodist2020-5)

Ash Wednesday is traditionally observed with fasting and abstinence from meat in several [Christian denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination").[[6]](#cite_note-ELCA1978-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Buchanan2015-7)[[8]](#cite_note-NHMC2021-8) As it is the first day of Lent, many Christians begin Ash Wednesday by marking a [Lenten calendar](/wiki/Lenten_calendar "Lenten calendar"), praying a Lenten [daily devotional](/wiki/Daily_devotional "Daily devotional"), and making a [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice") that they will not partake of until the arrival of [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide").[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-McDuff2013-10)

Many Christians attend special Ash Wednesday [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") at which churchgoers receive [ash](/wiki/Ash "Ash") on their foreheads or the top of their heads, as the wearing of ashes has been a sign of [repentance](/wiki/Repentance_in_Christianity "Repentance in Christianity") since [biblical](/wiki/Bible "Bible") times.[[11]](#cite_note-Hilovsky2024-11) The imposition of ashes is typically done with the [sign of the cross](/wiki/Sign_of_the_cross "Sign of the cross"), signifying that the recipient is a follower of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity").[[12]](#cite_note-TET2025-12) Ash Wednesday derives its name from this practice, in which the words accompany the placement (imposition) of ashes, "Repent, and believe in the Gospel" or the dictum "Remember that you are dust, and to dust you shall return."[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) The ashes are prepared by burning [palm leaves](/wiki/Palm_leaves "Palm leaves") from the previous year's [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") celebrations.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Observing and non-observing denominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Observing and non-observing denominations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Ash_Wednesday_Mass_at_Nazareth_Evangelical_Lutheran_Church.jpg/330px-Ash_Wednesday_Mass_at_Nazareth_Evangelical_Lutheran_Church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ash_Wednesday_Mass_at_Nazareth_Evangelical_Lutheran_Church.jpg)

The faithful receive ashes during the celebration of the [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") in an [Evangelical-Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") parish church.

Ash Wednesday is observed by numerous denominations within [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity").[[16]](#cite_note-Melton2011-16) [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"),[[note 1]](#cite_note-19) [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglicans "Anglicans"),[[16]](#cite_note-Melton2011-16) and [Moravians](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") observe it.[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) Churches in the [United Protestant](/wiki/United_Protestant "United Protestant") tradition, such as the [Church of North India](/wiki/Church_of_North_India "Church of North India"), [Church of South India](/wiki/Church_of_South_India "Church of South India"),[[21]](#cite_note-CSI_Ash_Wednesday_2019-22) and [United Church of Canada](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada") honour Ash Wednesday too.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

Some [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists"),[[23]](#cite_note-24) many [Methodists](/wiki/Methodists "Methodists") (including denominations aligned with the [holiness movement](/wiki/Holiness_movement "Holiness movement") such as [Nazarenes](/wiki/Church_of_the_Nazarene "Church of the Nazarene") and [Wesleyans](/wiki/Wesleyan_Church "Wesleyan Church")),[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) many [Radical Pietists](/wiki/Radical_Pietism "Radical Pietism") such as the [Evangelical Covenant Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Covenant_Church "Evangelical Covenant Church"),[[26]](#cite_note-27) and some [Conservative Anabaptists](/wiki/Conservative_Anabaptism "Conservative Anabaptism"), including some [Mennonites](/wiki/Mennonites "Mennonites"), observe Ash Wednesday.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29) The [Metropolitan Community Churches](/wiki/Metropolitan_Community_Church "Metropolitan Community Church") observe Ash Wednesday.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Many [Independent Catholics](/wiki/Independent_Catholics "Independent Catholics"),[[30]](#cite_note-Wagner2011-31)[[31]](#cite_note-Manila_Standard-32) and the [Community of Christ](/wiki/Community_of_Christ "Community of Christ") also observe it.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The [Reformed churches](/wiki/Reformed_church "Reformed church") have historically not observed Ash Wednesday, nor Lent in general, due to the Reformed [regulative principle of worship](/wiki/Regulative_principle_of_worship "Regulative principle of worship").[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36) Since the mid-twentieth century, many churches in the Reformed tradition (including certain [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist"), [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), and [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") churches) do observe both Ash Wednesday and Lent such as the [Church of Scotland](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland "Church of Scotland"), the [Protestant Church of the Netherlands](/wiki/Protestant_Church_of_the_Netherlands "Protestant Church of the Netherlands"), the [Swiss Reformed Church](/wiki/Swiss_Reformed_Church "Swiss Reformed Church"), the [Presbyterian Church (USA)](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_(USA) "Presbyterian Church (USA)"), and the [United Church of Christ](/wiki/United_Church_of_Christ "United Church of Christ").[[36]](#cite_note-EB1911-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-BartlettTaylor2009-39) The [Reformed Church in America](/wiki/Reformed_Church_in_America "Reformed Church in America"), for example, describes Ash Wednesday as a day "focused on prayer, fasting, and repentance."[[3]](#cite_note-2018RCA-3) The liturgy for Ash Wednesday thus contains the following "Invitation to Observe a Lenten Discipline" read by the presider:[[39]](#cite_note-RCA2018-40)

> We begin this holy season by acknowledging our need for repentance and our need for the love and forgiveness shown to us in Jesus Christ. I invite you, therefore, in the name of Christ, to observe a Holy Lent, by self-examination and penitence, by prayer and fasting, by practicing works of love, and by reading and reflecting on God's Holy Word.[[39]](#cite_note-RCA2018-40)

The [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") does not, in general, observe Ash Wednesday. Instead, Orthodox [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") begins on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday") and followed by Sad (Sorrowful) Wednesday.[[40]](#cite_note-EB2014-41) There are a relatively small number of Orthodox Christians who follow the [Western Rite](/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy "Western Rite Orthodoxy"). These do observe Ash Wednesday, although often on a different day from the previously mentioned denominations, as its date is determined from the Orthodox calculation of [Pascha](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), which may be as much as a month later than the Western observance of Easter.

## Observances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Observances")]

### Fasting and abstinence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Fasting and abstinence")]

Main articles: [Fasting and abstinence in the Catholic Church](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Catholic_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Catholic Church") and [Christian fasting](/wiki/Christian_fasting "Christian fasting")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Brooklyn_Museum_-_Jesus_Tempted_in_the_Wilderness_%28J%C3%A9sus_tent%C3%A9_dans_le_d%C3%A9sert%29_-_James_Tissot_-_overall.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Museum_-_Jesus_Tempted_in_the_Wilderness_%28J%C3%A9sus_tent%C3%A9_dans_le_d%C3%A9sert%29_-_James_Tissot_-_overall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_-_Jesus_Tempted_in_the_Wilderness_(J%C3%A9sus_tent%C3%A9_dans_le_d%C3%A9sert)_-_James_Tissot_-_overall.jpg)

*Jesus Tempted in the Wilderness* (*Jésus tenté dans le désert*), [James Tissot](/wiki/James_Tissot "James Tissot"), [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum")

Many Lent-observing denominations emphasize making a [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice"), as well as fasting and abstinence during the season of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), particularly on Ash Wednesday. The [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") spoke of Lent as a period of fasting for forty days in advance of Easter, although it is unclear whether the prescribed fast applied to all Christians, or specifically to [new Christians](/wiki/Conversion_to_Christianity "Conversion to Christianity") preparing to be [baptized](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism").[[41]](#cite_note-2011GassmannOldenburg-42)[[42]](#cite_note-Olsen-43) Whatever the council's original intent, this forty-day fast came into wide practice throughout the church.[[42]](#cite_note-Olsen-43)

While starting a Lenten sacrifice on Ash Wednesday (e.g. giving up alcohol), it is customary to [pray](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") for strength to keep it through the whole season of Lent; many often wish others to do so as well, e.g. "May God bless your Lenten sacrifice."[[43]](#cite_note-44) In many places, Christians historically abstained from food for a whole day until the evening, and at sunset, Western Christians traditionally broke the Lenten fast, which is often known as the [Black Fast](/wiki/Black_Fast "Black Fast").[[44]](#cite_note-Cléir2017-45)[[45]](#cite_note-GuérangerFromage1912-46) In [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), many Christians continue this practice of [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") until sunset on Ash Wednesday and Good Friday, with some fasting in this manner throughout the whole season of Lent.[[46]](#cite_note-47) In India, Ash Wednesday is called व्रत विभूति (*Vrat Vibhuti* - meaning, "the sacred ash of the Holy Fast"). After attending a worship service, often on Wednesday evenings, it is common for Christians of various denominations that celebrate Lent to break that day's Lenten fast together through a communal [Lenten supper](/wiki/Lenten_supper "Lenten supper"), which is held in the church's [parish hall](/wiki/Parish_hall "Parish hall").[[47]](#cite_note-Lighthouse2018-48)

Among Catholics, Ash Wednesday is observed by [fasting](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Catholic_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Catholic Church"), [abstinence](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Catholic_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Catholic Church") from meat (which begins at age 14 according to canon law 1252[[48]](#cite_note-49)), and [repentance](/wiki/Repentance_in_Christianity "Repentance in Christianity"). On Ash Wednesday and [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), Roman Catholics between the ages of 18 and 59, whose health enables them to fast, are permitted to consume one full meal, along with two smaller meals, which together should not equal the full meal. Some Catholics will go beyond the minimum obligations put forth by the Church and undertake a complete fast or a bread and water fast until sunset. Ash Wednesday and Good Friday are also days of abstinence from meat ([mammals](/wiki/Mammals "Mammals") and [fowl](/wiki/Fowl "Fowl")), as are all Fridays during Lent.[[49]](#cite_note-50) Some Roman Catholics continue fasting throughout Lent, as was the Church's traditional requirement,[[50]](#cite_note-51) concluding only after the celebration of the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil"). Where the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite") is observed, the day of fasting and abstinence is postponed to the first Friday in the Ambrosian Lent, nine days later.[[51]](#cite_note-Thouret-52)

Several [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") parishes teach communicants to fast on Ash Wednesday, with some parishioners choosing to continue doing so throughout the entire season of Lent, especially on Good Friday.[[52]](#cite_note-Hatch1978-53)[[53]](#cite_note-GassmannOldenburg2011-54)[[54]](#cite_note-Pfatteicher1990-55)[[55]](#cite_note-JacobsHaas1899-56) *A Handbook for the Discipline of Lent*, a Lutheran liturgical manual, recommends that the faithful "Fast on Ash Wednesday and Good Friday with only one simple meal during the day, usually without meat".[[6]](#cite_note-ELCA1978-6)

In the Church of England, and throughout much of the Worldwide Anglican Communion, the entire forty days of Lent are designated days of fasting. Fridays are designated as days of abstinence in the [1662 *Book of Common Prayer*](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer_(1662) "Book of Common Prayer (1662)").[[7]](#cite_note-Buchanan2015-7) *[Saint Augustine's Prayer Book](/wiki/Saint_Augustine%27s_Prayer_Book "Saint Augustine's Prayer Book")*, a resource for Anglicans of Anglo-Catholic churchmanship, defines "Fasting" as "usually meaning not more than a light breakfast, one full meal, and one-half meal, on the forty days of Lent."[[56]](#cite_note-Gavitt1991-57) The same text defines [abstinence](/wiki/Abstinence "Abstinence") as refraining from flesh meat on all Fridays of the Church Year, except for those during [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide").[[56]](#cite_note-Gavitt1991-57)

In the Methodist tradition, [John Wesley's sermons](/wiki/Sermons_on_Several_Occasions "Sermons on Several Occasions") on the topic of the [Sermon on the Mount](/wiki/Sermon_on_the_Mount "Sermon on the Mount") stress the importance of the Lenten fast, which begins on Ash Wednesday.[[57]](#cite_note-AbrahamKirby2009-58) The United Methodist Church therefore states that:

> There is a strong biblical base for fasting, particularly during the 40 days of Lent leading to the celebration of Easter. Jesus, as part of his spiritual preparation, went into the wilderness and fasted 40 days and 40 nights, according to the Gospels.[[58]](#cite_note-UMCFasting2017-59)

Rev. Jacqui King, the minister of Nu Faith Community United Methodist Church in Houston explained the philosophy of fasting during Lent as "I'm not skipping a meal because in place of that meal, I'm dining with God".[[59]](#cite_note-60)

Members of the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church") may voluntarily fast during the season of Lent, along with making a [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice") for the season as a form of penitence.[[8]](#cite_note-NHMC2021-8)

### Date of Ash Wednesday

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Date of Ash Wednesday")]

Ash Wednesday is always 46 days before Easter. Easter is determined as the Sunday following the first full moon that happens on or after the March equinox (which is always 21 March).[[60]](#cite_note-61)

Lent is 40 days long, not including Sundays. According to the calendar, that means the season is 46 days long overall. Lent begins on Ash Wednesday and ends on [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") (in the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"),[[61]](#cite_note-62) [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheran_World_Federation "Lutheran World Federation"),[[62]](#cite_note-Northwestern_Publishing_House-63) [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"),[[63]](#cite_note-Kitch_130-64) [Methodist Church](/wiki/World_Methodist_Council "World Methodist Council"),[[64]](#cite_note-Langford_96-65) [Reformed Churches](/wiki/Reformed_Church "Reformed Church") {[Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") and [Congregationalist](/wiki/Congregationalist "Congregationalist")},[[65]](#cite_note-66) [Western Rite Orthodox Church](/wiki/Western-Rite_Orthodox_Churches "Western-Rite Orthodox Churches"),[[66]](#cite_note-Fenton2014-67) and [United Protestant Churches](/wiki/United_Protestant_Church "United Protestant Church")[[67]](#cite_note-68)) or at the start of the [Easter Triduum](/wiki/Easter_Triduum "Easter Triduum") on the evening of [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday") in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (though the Lenten fasting discipline ends on the evening of Holy Saturday, as with the Lutheran Churches).[[68]](#cite_note-EWTN-69)

### Ashes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Ashes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/US_Navy_060301-N-5686B-001_USS_Kitty_Hawk_%28CV_63%29%2C_Chaplain%2C_Lt._Matthias_Rendon_blesses_ashes%2C_during_an_Ash_Wednesday_service_held_on_board_Commander_Fleet_Activities_Yokosuka.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_060301-N-5686B-001_USS_Kitty_Hawk_(CV_63),_Chaplain,_Lt._Matthias_Rendon_blesses_ashes,_during_an_Ash_Wednesday_service_held_on_board_Commander_Fleet_Activities_Yokosuka.jpg)

A priest blesses ashes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Ash_Wednesday_at_the_Manila_Cathedral_2026.jpg/250px-Ash_Wednesday_at_the_Manila_Cathedral_2026.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ash_Wednesday_at_the_Manila_Cathedral_2026.jpg)

A [lay minister](/wiki/Laity "Laity") draws a cross of ashes on a worshipper's forehead, the prevailing form in [English-speaking countries](/wiki/English-speaking_countries "English-speaking countries").[[69]](#cite_note-McNamara-70)

Ashes are ceremonially placed on the heads of Christians on Ash Wednesday, either by being sprinkled over their heads or, in English-speaking countries, more often by being marked on their foreheads as a visible cross. The words (based on [Genesis](/wiki/Book_of_Genesis "Book of Genesis") 3:19)[[70]](#cite_note-71) used traditionally to accompany this gesture are, "*Memento, homo, quia pulvis es, et in pulverem reverteris.*" ("Remember, man, that thou art dust, and to dust thou shalt return.") This custom is credited to [Pope Gregory I](/wiki/Pope_Gregory_I "Pope Gregory I") the Great (c. 540 – 604),[[42]](#cite_note-Olsen-43) although this is probably incorrect since Ash Wednesday was not part of Lent in his time.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

In the [1969 missal](/wiki/Mass_of_Paul_VI "Mass of Paul VI") of the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"), an alternative formula (based on [Mark 1](/wiki/Mark_1 "Mark 1"):15)[[72]](#cite_note-73) was introduced and given first place "Repent, and believe in the Gospel" and the older formula was translated as "Remember that you are dust, and to dust you shall return." The old formula, based on the words spoken to [Adam and Eve](/wiki/Adam_and_Eve "Adam and Eve") after [their sin](/wiki/Fall_of_man "Fall of man"),[[73]](#cite_note-74) reminds worshippers of their sinfulness and mortality and thus, implicitly, of their need to repent in time.[[74]](#cite_note-Bucher-75)

Various manners of placing the ashes on worshippers' heads are in use within the Latin Church, the two most common being to use the ashes to make a cross on the forehead and sprinkle the ashes over the crown of the head. Originally, the ashes were strewn over men's heads, but, probably because [women had their heads covered in church](/wiki/Head_covering_for_Christian_women "Head covering for Christian women") (based on [1 Corinthians 11](/wiki/First_Epistle_to_the_Corinthians "First Epistle to the Corinthians")),[[75]](#cite_note-76) were placed on the foreheads of women.[[76]](#cite_note-77) In the Catholic Church the manner of imposing ashes depends largely on local custom since no fixed rule has been laid down.[[69]](#cite_note-McNamara-70) In the [Lutheran Churches](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism"), ashes are normatively placed on the forehead in the shape of a cross.[[77]](#cite_note-78)

Although the account of [Ælfric of Eynsham](/wiki/%C3%86lfric_of_Eynsham "Ælfric of Eynsham") shows that in about the year 1000 the ashes were "strewn" on the head,[[78]](#cite_note-strew-79) the marking of the forehead is the method that now prevails in English-speaking countries and is the only one envisaged in the *Occasional Offices* of the [Anglican Church of Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Papua_New_Guinea "Anglican Church of Papua New Guinea"), a publication described as "noticeably Anglo-Catholic in character".[[79]](#cite_note-blessing-80) In its ritual of "Blessing of Ashes", this states that "the ashes are blessed at the beginning of the Eucharist. After they have been blessed they are placed on the forehead of the clergy and people."[[79]](#cite_note-blessing-80)
The Ash Wednesday ritual of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"), Mother Church of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), contains "The Imposition of Ashes" in its Ash Wednesday liturgy.[[80]](#cite_note-CofE-81) On Ash Wednesday, the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope"), the [Bishop of Rome](/wiki/Bishop_of_Rome "Bishop of Rome"), traditionally takes part in a [penitential procession](/wiki/Penitential_procession "Penitential procession") from the [Church of Saint Anselm](/wiki/Church_of_Sant%27Anselmo_all%27Aventino,_Rome "Church of Sant'Anselmo all'Aventino, Rome") to the Basilica of [Santa Sabina](/wiki/Santa_Sabina "Santa Sabina"), where, by the custom in Italy and many other countries, ashes are sprinkled on his head, not smudged on his forehead, and he places ashes on the heads of others in the same way.[[81]](#cite_note-82)

The Anglican ritual, used in Papua New Guinea states that, after the blessing of the ashes, "the priest marks his forehead and then the foreheads of the [servers](/wiki/Acolyte "Acolyte") and congregation who come and kneel, or stand, where they normally receive the Blessed Sacrament."[[79]](#cite_note-blessing-80) The corresponding Catholic ritual in the [Roman Missal](/wiki/Roman_Missal "Roman Missal") for celebration within [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") merely states: "Then the Priest places ashes on the head of those present who come to him, and says to each one ..."[[82]](#cite_note-RM-83) Pre-1970 editions had much more elaborate instructions about the order in which the participants were to receive the ashes, but again without any indication of the form of placing the ashes on the head.[[83]](#cite_note-TRM-84)

The 1969 revision of the Roman Rite inserted into the Mass the solemn ceremony of blessing ashes and placing them on heads, but also explicitly envisaged a similar solemn ceremony outside of Mass.[[82]](#cite_note-RM-83) The Book of Blessings contains a simple rite.[[69]](#cite_note-McNamara-70) While the solemn rite would normally be carried out within a church building, the simple rite could appropriately be used almost anywhere. While only a priest or deacon may bless the ashes, laypeople may do the placing of the ashes on a person's head. Even in the solemn rite, laymen or women may assist the priest in distributing the ashes. In addition, laypeople take blessed ashes left over after the collective ceremony and place them on the heads of the sick or of others who are unable to attend the blessing.[[69]](#cite_note-McNamara-70)[[84]](#cite_note-CUF-85) (In 2014, Anglican [Liverpool Cathedral](/wiki/Liverpool_Cathedral "Liverpool Cathedral") likewise offered to impose ashes within the church without a solemn ceremony.)[[85]](#cite_note-86)

In addition, those who attend such Catholic services, whether in a church or elsewhere, traditionally take blessed ashes home with them to place on the heads of other members of the family,[[86]](#cite_note-87) and it is recommended to have envelopes available to facilitate this practice.[[87]](#cite_note-K&L-88) At home the ashes are then placed with little or no ceremony.

Unlike its discipline regarding [sacraments](/wiki/Sacraments_of_the_Catholic_Church "Sacraments of the Catholic Church"), the Catholic Church does not exclude anyone from receiving [sacramentals](/wiki/Sacramentals "Sacramentals"), such as the placing of ashes on the head, even those who are not Catholics and perhaps not even baptized.[[84]](#cite_note-CUF-85) Even those who have been [excommunicated](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication") and are therefore forbidden to *celebrate* sacramentals are not forbidden to *receive* them.[[88]](#cite_note-89) After describing the blessing, the rite of Blessing and Distribution of Ashes (within Mass) states: "Then the Priest places ashes on the heads of all those present who come to him."[[82]](#cite_note-RM-83) The Catholic Church does not limit the distribution of blessed ashes to church buildings and has suggested the holding of celebrations in shopping centers, nursing homes, and factories.[[87]](#cite_note-K&L-88) Such celebrations presume preparation of an appropriate area and include readings from Scripture (at least one) and prayers, and are somewhat shorter if the ashes are already blessed.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

The Catholic Church and the Methodist Church say that the ashes should be those of palm branches blessed at the previous year's [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") service,[[82]](#cite_note-RM-83)[[90]](#cite_note-91) while a Church of England publication says they "may be made" from the burnt palm crosses of the previous year.[[79]](#cite_note-blessing-80)[[80]](#cite_note-CofE-81) These sources do not speak of adding anything to the ashes other than, for the Catholic liturgy, a sprinkling with holy water when blessing them. An Anglican website speaks of mixing the ashes with a small amount of holy water or [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") as a fixative.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

Where ashes are placed on the head by smudging the forehead with a sign of the cross, many Christians choose to keep the mark visible throughout the day. The churches have not imposed this as an obligatory rule, and the ashes may even be wiped off immediately after receiving them,[[92]](#cite_note-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94) but some Christian leaders, such as Lutheran pastor Richard P. Bucher and Catholic bishop Kieran Conry, recommend keeping the ashes on the forehead for the rest of the day as a public profession of the Christian faith.[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96) Morgan Guyton, a Methodist pastor, and leader in the [Red-Letter Christian](/wiki/Red-Letter_Christian "Red-Letter Christian") movement, encourages Christians to wear their ashed cross throughout the day as an exercise of [religious freedom](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion").[[96]](#cite_note-97)

#### Ashes to Go

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ashes to Go")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Ashes_to_Go_at_Mizner_Park.jpg/250px-Ashes_to_Go_at_Mizner_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ashes_to_Go_at_Mizner_Park.jpg)

Two [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") priests distribute ashes to passersby in the American city of [Boca Raton](/wiki/Boca_Raton,_Florida "Boca Raton, Florida") as part of the Ashes to Go movement.

Since 2007, some members of major Christian Churches in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), including Anglicans, Lutherans, United Church of Christ, and Methodists, have participated in 'Ashes to Go' activities, in which clergy go outside of their [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") to public places, such as [city centres](/wiki/Downtown "Downtown"), [sidewalks](/wiki/Sidewalk "Sidewalk") and [railroad stations](/wiki/Railroad_station "Railroad station"), to distribute ashes to passers-by,[[97]](#cite_note-CatholicHerald2016-98)[[98]](#cite_note-Grossman-99)[[99]](#cite_note-100) even to people waiting in their cars for a stoplight to change.[[100]](#cite_note-The_United_Methodist_Church-101) The Anglican priest Emily Mellott of Calvary Church in [Lombard](/wiki/Lombard,_Illinois "Lombard, Illinois") took up the idea and turned it into a movement, stated that the practice was also an act of [evangelism](/wiki/Evangelism "Evangelism").[[101]](#cite_note-AshesToGo-102)[[102]](#cite_note-103)

Anglicans and Catholics in parts of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") such as [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland,_Tyne_and_Wear "Sunderland, Tyne and Wear"), are offering Ashes to Go together: Marc Lyden-Smith, the priest of [Saint Mary's Church](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Church,_Sunderland "St Mary's Church, Sunderland"), stated that the [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenical "Ecumenical") effort is a "tremendous witness in our city, with Catholics and Anglicans working together to start the season of Lent, perhaps reminding those who have [fallen away](/wiki/Fallen_away "Fallen away") from the Church, or have never been before, that the Christian faith is alive and active in Sunderland."[[97]](#cite_note-CatholicHerald2016-98) The Catholic Student Association of [Kent State University](/wiki/Kent_State_University "Kent State University"), based at the University Parish Newman Center, offered ashes to university students who were going through the Student Center of that institution in 2012,[[103]](#cite_note-104) and Douglas Clark of St. Matthew's Roman Catholic Church in [Statesboro](/wiki/Statesboro,_Georgia "Statesboro, Georgia"), among others, have participated in Ashes to Go.[[104]](#cite_note-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106)

On Ash Wednesday 2017, Father Paddy Mooney, the priest of St Patrick's Roman Catholic Church in the Irish town of [Glenamaddy](/wiki/Glenamaddy "Glenamaddy"), set up an Ashes to Go station through which commuters could drive and receive ashes from their car; the parish church also had "drive-through prayers during Lent with people submitting requests into a box left in the church grounds without having to leave their car".[[106]](#cite_note-107) Reverend Trey Hall, pastor of Urban Village United Methodist Church, stated that when his local church offered ashes in Chicago "nearly 300 people received ashes – including two people who were waiting in their car for a stoplight to change."[[100]](#cite_note-The_United_Methodist_Church-101)

In 2013, churches not only in the United States but also at least one church each in the United Kingdom, Canada, and South Africa, participated in Ashes to Go.[[107]](#cite_note-108) Outside of their [church building](/wiki/Church_building "Church building"), Saint Stephen Martyr Lutheran Church in [Canton](/wiki/Canton,_Ohio "Canton, Ohio") offered Ashes to Go for "believers whose schedules make it difficult to attend a traditional service" in 2016.[[108]](#cite_note-109) In the United States itself 34 states and the District of Columbia had at least one [church](/wiki/Church_building "Church building") taking part. Most of these churches (parishes) were Episcopal, but there were also several Methodist churches, as well as Presbyterian and Catholic churches.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

### Commination Office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Commination Office")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/AshWednesdayAltar.jpg/250px-AshWednesdayAltar.jpg)](/wiki/File:AshWednesdayAltar.jpg)

St. Mary's Episcopal Cathedral in Memphis, Tennessee, on Ash Wednesday 2011. The veiled [altar cross](/wiki/Altar_cross "Altar cross") and purple paraments are customary during Lent.

Robin Knowles Wallace states that the traditional Ash Wednesday [church service](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") includes Psalm 51 (the *Miserere*), prayers of confession, and the sign of ashes.[[110]](#cite_note-Wallace2010-111) No single one of the traditional services contains all of these elements. The Anglican church's traditional Ash Wednesday service, titled *A Commination*,[[111]](#cite_note-EnglandMant1825-112) contains the first two elements, but not the third. On the other hand, the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church")'s traditional service has the blessing and distribution of ashes but, while prayers of confession and recitation of Psalm 51 (the first psalm at [Lauds](/wiki/Lauds "Lauds") on all penitential days, including Ash Wednesday) are a part of its general traditional Ash Wednesday liturgy,[[112]](#cite_note-Sweeney-113) they are not associated specifically with the rite of blessing the ashes.[[113]](#cite_note-L'abbaye_Saint_Pierre_de_Solesmes-114)

The rite of blessing has acquired an untraditionally weak association with that particular psalm only since 1970 when it was inserted into the celebration of Mass, at which a few verses of Psalm 51 are used as a [responsorial psalm](/wiki/Psalms#Catholic_usage "Psalms"). Where the traditional Gregorian Chants are still used, the psalm continues to enjoy a prominent place in the ceremony.[[113]](#cite_note-L'abbaye_Saint_Pierre_de_Solesmes-114)

In the mid-16th century, the first [Book of Common Prayer](/wiki/Book_of_Common_Prayer "Book of Common Prayer") removed the ceremony of the ashes from the liturgy of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and replaced it with what would later be called the Commination Office.[[114]](#cite_note-115) In that 1549 edition, the rite was headed: "The First Day of Lent: Commonly Called Ash-Wednesday".[[115]](#cite_note-EnglandMant1825A-116) The ashes ceremony was not forbidden, but was not included in the church's official liturgy.[[36]](#cite_note-EB1911-37) Its place was taken by reading biblical curses of God against sinners, to each of which the people were directed to respond with Amen.[[116]](#cite_note-117)[[117]](#cite_note-118)

The text of the "Commination or Denouncing of God's Anger and Judgments against Sinners" begins: "In the primitive Church there was a [godly discipline](/wiki/Godly_discipline "Godly discipline"), that, at the beginning of Lent, such persons as stood convicted of notorious sin were put to open penance, and punished in this world, that their souls might be saved in the day of the Lord; and that others, admonished by their example, might be the more afraid to offend. Instead whereof, until the said discipline may be restored, (which is much to be wished,) it is thought good that at this time (in the presence of you all) should be read the general sentences of God's cursing against impenitent sinners".[[118]](#cite_note-119) In line with this, Joseph Hooper Maude wrote that the establishment of *The Commination* was due to a desire of the reformers "to restore the primitive practice of public penance in church". He further stated that "the sentences of the greater excommunication" within *The Commination* corresponded to those used in the [ancient Church](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity").[[119]](#cite_note-Maude1901-120)

The Anglican Church's Ash Wednesday liturgy, he wrote, also traditionally included the *[Miserere](/wiki/Psalm_51 "Psalm 51")*, which, along with "what follows" in the rest of the service (lesser Litany, Lord's Prayer, three prayers for pardon and final blessing), "was taken from the [Sarum services](/wiki/Sarum_Use "Sarum Use") for Ash Wednesday".[[119]](#cite_note-Maude1901-120) From the Sarum Rite practice in England the service took Psalm 51 and some prayers that in the Sarum Missal accompanied the blessing and distribution of ashes.[[112]](#cite_note-Sweeney-113)[[120]](#cite_note-121) In the Sarum Rite, the *Miserere* psalm was one of the seven penitential psalms that were recited at the beginning of the ceremony.[[121]](#cite_note-122) In the 20th century, the Episcopal Church introduced three prayers from the Sarum Rite and omitted the Commination Office from its liturgy.[[36]](#cite_note-EB1911-37)

### Low church ceremonies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Low church ceremonies")]

In some of the [low church](/wiki/Low_church "Low church") traditions, other practices are sometimes added or substituted, as other ways of symbolizing the confession and penitence of the day. For example, in one common variation, small cards are distributed to the congregation on which people are invited to write a sin they wish to confess. These small cards are brought forth to the [altar table](/wiki/Altar "Altar") where they are burned.[[122]](#cite_note-123)

### Regional customs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Regional customs")]

In the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era"), theatres refrained from presenting costumed shows on Ash Wednesday, so they provided other entertainment, as mandated by the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") (Anglican Church).[[123]](#cite_note-124)

In [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), children "pin small bags of ashes on the back of some unsuspecting person",[[124]](#cite_note-Lacy2000-125) dress up in costumes, and sing songs for candy.[[125]](#cite_note-126)

In [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") if someone in the village didn't receive ash, they "shared their blessing" by rubbing their foreheads together, and the [Csángó](/wiki/Cs%C3%A1ng%C3%B3s "Csángós") people of Moldova even cover the pots with ashes to bring good luck. An interesting related tradition is that since dancing was forbidden, children played dancing games (like ulicskázás and hajujvárazás) and ball games (as mancsozás, csülgözés, kutyasatú and sajbózás), all specific to Hungary.[[126]](#cite_note-127)

## Biblical significance of ashes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Biblical significance of ashes")]

Ashes were used in ancient times to express grief or repentance.

The biblical text of [Jonah 3](/wiki/Jonah_3 "Jonah 3") holds that after the prophet Jonah warned those inhabiting [Nineveh](/wiki/Nineveh "Nineveh") for the second time, its inhabitants repented, fasting from food and water, along with wearing ashes and [sackcloth](/wiki/Sackcloth "Sackcloth").[[127]](#cite_note-Bromiley1979-128) For this reason, God spared the [Ninevites](/wiki/Ninevites "Ninevites") according to the text (cf. *[Fast of Nineveh](/wiki/Fast_of_Nineveh "Fast of Nineveh")*).[[127]](#cite_note-Bromiley1979-128)

When [Tamar](/wiki/Tamar_(daughter_of_David) "Tamar (daughter of David)") was raped by her half-brother, "she sprinkled ashes on her head, tore her robe, and with her face buried in her hands went away crying" ([2 Samuel 13:19](https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=2+Samuel+13:19&version=gnt)). The gesture was also used to express sorrow for sins and faults. Ashes could be symbolic of the old sinful self dying and returning to the dust. In [Job](/wiki/Book_of_Job "Book of Job") [42:5–6](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Job#42:5 "s:Bible (King James)/Job"), Job says to God: "I have heard of thee by the hearing of the ear: but now mine eye seeth thee. Wherefore I abhor myself, and repent in dust and ashes."

The prophet Jeremiah calls for repentance by saying: "O daughter of my people, gird on sackcloth, roll in the ashes" (Jer 6:26). The prophet Daniel recounted pleading to God: "I turned to the Lord God, pleading in earnest prayer, with fasting, sackcloth, and ashes" (Daniel 9:3). Just before the New Testament period, the rebels fighting for Jewish independence, the [Maccabees](/wiki/Maccabees "Maccabees"), prepared for battle using ashes: "That day they fasted and wore sackcloth; they sprinkled ashes on their heads and tore their clothes" (1 Maccabees 3:47; see also 4:39).

Examples of the practice among Jews are found in several other [books of the Bible](/wiki/Books_of_the_Bible "Books of the Bible"), including [Numbers](/wiki/Book_of_Numbers "Book of Numbers") [19:9](https://mechon-mamre.org/p/pt/pt0419.htm#9), [19:17](https://mechon-mamre.org/p/pt/pt0419.htm#17), [Jonah](/wiki/Book_of_Jonah "Book of Jonah") [3:6](https://mechon-mamre.org/p/pt/pt1703.htm#6), [Book of Esther](/wiki/Book_of_Esther "Book of Esther") [4:1](https://www.esv.org/Esther+4:1), and [Hebrews](/wiki/Epistle_to_the_Hebrews "Epistle to the Hebrews") [9:13](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Hebrews#9:13 "s:Bible (King James)/Hebrews"). Jesus is quoted as speaking of the practice in [Matthew](/wiki/Gospel_of_Matthew "Gospel of Matthew") [11:21](https://www.esv.org/Matthew+11:21) and [Luke](/wiki/Gospel_of_Luke "Gospel of Luke") [10:13](https://www.esv.org/Luke+10:13): "If the mighty works done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago (sitting) in sackcloth and ashes."

## Christian use of ashes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Christian use of ashes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Fa%C5%82at_Julian%2C_Popielec.jpg/250px-Fa%C5%82at_Julian%2C_Popielec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fa%C5%82at_Julian,_Popielec.jpg)

An 1881 Polish painting of a Roman Catholic priest sprinkling ashes on the heads of worshippers, the method prevailing in Italy, Poland, Spain, and parts of Latin America.[[69]](#cite_note-McNamara-70)

Christians continued the practice of using ashes as an external sign of repentance. [Tertullian](/wiki/Tertullian "Tertullian") (c. 160 – c. 225) said that confession of sin should be accompanied by lying in [sackcloth](/wiki/Sackcloth "Sackcloth") and ashes.[[128]](#cite_note-129) The historian [Eusebius](/wiki/Eusebius "Eusebius") (c. 260/265 – 339/340) recounts how a repentant apostate covered himself with ashes when begging [Pope Zephyrinus](/wiki/Pope_Zephyrinus "Pope Zephyrinus") to readmit him to communion.[[129]](#cite_note-130)

John W. Fenton writes that "by the end of the 10th century, it was customary in Western Europe (but not yet in Rome) for all the faithful to receive ashes on the first day of the Lenten fast. In 1091, this custom was then ordered by Pope Urban II at the council of Benevento to be extended to the church in Rome. Not long after that, the name of the day was referred to in the liturgical books as "Feria Quarta Cinerum" (i.e., Ash Wednesday)."[[130]](#cite_note-Fenton-131)

The public penance that grave sinners underwent before being admitted to Holy Communion just before Easter lasted throughout [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), on the first day of which they were sprinkled with ashes and dressed in sackcloth. When, towards the end of the first millennium, the discipline of public penance was dropped, the beginning of Lent, seen as a general penitential season, was marked by sprinkling ashes on the heads of all.[[40]](#cite_note-EB2014-41) This practice is found in the [Gregorian Sacramentary](/wiki/Sacramentary#The_Gregorian_Sacramentary "Sacramentary") of the late 8th century.[[74]](#cite_note-Bucher-75)[[131]](#cite_note-132) About two centuries later, [Ælfric of Eynsham](/wiki/%C3%86lfric_of_Eynsham "Ælfric of Eynsham"), an Anglo-Saxon abbot, wrote of the rite of strewing ashes on heads at the start of Lent.[[78]](#cite_note-strew-79)[[132]](#cite_note-133)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Carl_Spitzweg_-_Aschermittwoch.jpg/250px-Carl_Spitzweg_-_Aschermittwoch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carl_Spitzweg_-_Aschermittwoch.jpg)

*Ash Wednesday* by [Carl Spitzweg](/wiki/Carl_Spitzweg "Carl Spitzweg"): the end of [Carnival](/wiki/Carnival "Carnival")

The article on Ash Wednesday in the  [*Encyclopædia Britannica* Eleventh Edition](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition "Encyclopædia Britannica Eleventh Edition") states that, after the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), the ashes ceremony was not forbidden in the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England"); liturgical scholar Blair Meeks notes that the Lutheran and Anglican denominations "never lapsed in this observance".[[133]](#cite_note-Meeks2003-134) It was even prescribed under King [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") in 1538 and under King [Edward VI](/wiki/Edward_VI_of_England "Edward VI of England") in 1550, but it fell out of use in many areas after 1600.[[36]](#cite_note-EB1911-37) In 1536, the [Ten Articles](/wiki/Ten_Articles "Ten Articles") issued by authority of Henry VIII commended "the observance of various rites and ceremonies as good and laudable, such as clerical vestments, a sprinkling of holy water, bearing of candles on Candlemas-day, giving of ashes on Ash-Wednesday".[[134]](#cite_note-Schaff1877-135)

After Henry's death in January 1547, [Thomas Cranmer](/wiki/Thomas_Cranmer "Thomas Cranmer"), within the same year, "procured an order from the Council to forbid the carrying of candles on Candlemas-day, and the use of ashes on Ash-Wednesday, and of palms on Palm-Sunday, as superstitious ceremonies", an order that was issued only for the ecclesiastical province of Canterbury, of which Cranmer was archbishop.[[135]](#cite_note-136)[[136]](#cite_note-137)[[137]](#cite_note-Foxe-138) *The Church Cyclopædia* states that the "English office had adapted the very old [Salisbury service](/wiki/Sarum_Rite "Sarum Rite") for Ash-Wednesday, prefacing it with an address and a recital of the curses of Mount Ebal, and then with an exhortation uses the older service very nearly as it stood."[[119]](#cite_note-Maude1901-120)[[138]](#cite_note-Benton1883-139)

The new Commination Office had no blessing of ashes and therefore, in England as a whole, "soon after the Reformation, the use of ashes was discontinued as a 'vain show' and Ash Wednesday then became only a day of marked solemnity, with a memorial of its original character in a reading of the curses denounced against impenitent sinners".[[139]](#cite_note-140) The Protestant Episcopal Church in the United States of America, in the 19th century, observed Ash Wednesday: "as a day of fasting and humiliation, wherein we are publicly to confess our sins, meekly to implore God's mercy and forgiveness, and humbly to intercede for the continuance of his favour".[[140]](#cite_note-141) In the 20th century, the Book of Common Prayer provided prayers for the imposition of ashes.[[141]](#cite_note-PublishingChurch1979-142)

Monte Canfield and Blair Meeks state that after the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation"), Lutherans and Anglicans kept the rite of blessing and distributing ashes to the faithful on Ash Wednesday, and that the Protestant denominations that did not keep it, such as the Methodists, encouraged its use "during and after the ecumenical era that resulted in the Vatican II proclamations".[[133]](#cite_note-Meeks2003-134)[[142]](#cite_note-Canfield-143) [Jack Kingsbury](/wiki/Jack_Dean_Kingsbury "Jack Dean Kingsbury") and Russell F. Anderson likewise state that the practice was continued among some Lutherans and Anglicans.[[143]](#cite_note-KingsburyPennington1980-144)[[144]](#cite_note-Anderson1996-145)

As part of the [liturgical revival](/wiki/Liturgical_Movement "Liturgical Movement") ushered in by the [ecumenical movement](/wiki/Ecumenical_movement "Ecumenical movement"), the practice was encouraged in Protestant churches,[[142]](#cite_note-Canfield-143) including the [Methodist Church](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism").[[145]](#cite_note-Denominations_1-146)[[146]](#cite_note-147) It has also been adopted by [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist") and [Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed_church "Continental Reformed church") churches and some less liturgical [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denominations "Christian denominations").[[147]](#cite_note-Brumley-148)

The [Eastern Orthodox churches](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") generally do not observe Ash Wednesday,[[40]](#cite_note-EB2014-41) although in recent times, the creation of the [Antiochian Western Rite Vicariate](/wiki/Antiochian_Western_Rite_Vicariate "Antiochian Western Rite Vicariate") has led to the observance of Ash Wednesday among [Western Orthodox](/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy "Western Rite Orthodoxy") parishes.[[130]](#cite_note-Fenton-131) In this tradition, ashes "may be distributed outside of the mass or any liturgical service" although "commonly the faithful receive their ashes immediately before the Ash Wednesday mass".[[130]](#cite_note-Fenton-131) In Orthodoxy, historically, "serious public sinners in the East also donned sackcloth, including those who made the Great Fast a major theme of their entire lives such as hermits and desert-dwellers."[[148]](#cite_note-149) [Byzantine Rite Catholics](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), although in the United States use "the same Gregorian calendar as the Roman Catholic rite", do not practice the distribution of ashes as it is "not part of their ancient tradition".[[149]](#cite_note-150)

In the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite"), ashes are blessed and placed on the heads of the faithful not on the day that elsewhere is called Ash Wednesday, but at the end of Mass on the following Sunday, which in that rite inaugurates Lent, with the fast traditionally beginning on Monday, the first weekday of the Ambrosian Lent.[[150]](#cite_note-151)[[51]](#cite_note-Thouret-52)[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153)

## Dates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Dates")]

Further information: [Lenten calendar](/wiki/Lenten_calendar "Lenten calendar")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Lent_calendar.svg/500px-Lent_calendar.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lent_calendar.svg)

*Ash Wednesday* and other named days and day ranges around Lent and Easter in Western Christianity, with the fasting days of Lent numbered

Ash Wednesday is exactly 46 days before Easter Sunday, a [moveable feast](/wiki/Moveable_feast "Moveable feast") [based on the cycles of the moon](/wiki/Computus "Computus"). The earliest date Ash Wednesday can occur is 4 February (which is only possible during a common year with Easter Sunday on 22 March), which happened in 1598, 1693, 1761, and 1818 and will next occur in 2285.[[153]](#cite_note-154) The latest date Ash Wednesday can occur is 10 March (when Easter Sunday falls on 25 April) which occurred in 1666, 1734, 1886 and 1943 and will next occur in 2038.

Since the introduction of the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") in 1582, Ash Wednesday has never occurred on a Leap Year Day (29 February) but it will do so for the first time in 2096. The only other years of the third millennium that will have Ash Wednesday on 29 February are 2468, 2688, 2840, and 2992.[[154]](#cite_note-155) (Ash Wednesday falls on 29 February if and only if Easter is on 15 April in a [leap year](/wiki/Leap_year_starting_on_Sunday "Leap year starting on Sunday").) Also, there are certain times that Ash Wednesday coincides with [Valentine's Day](/wiki/Valentine%27s_Day "Valentine's Day") (14 February), which occurred in 1923, 1934, 1945, 2018, 2024, and will next occur in 2029.[[155]](#cite_note-156)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/High_Altar_of_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church_during_Lent.jpg/250px-High_Altar_of_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church_during_Lent.jpg)](/wiki/File:High_Altar_of_Palmer_Memorial_Episcopal_Church_during_Lent.jpg)

The [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") of a church on Ash Wednesday 2015 (the veiled [altar cross](/wiki/Altar_cross "Altar cross") and purple [paraments](/wiki/Parament "Parament") are customary during Lent)

Ash Wednesday marks the start of a 40-day period which is an allusion to the separation of Jesus in the desert to [fast](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") and [pray](/wiki/Prayer "Prayer"). During this time he was [tempted](/wiki/Temptation_of_Christ "Temptation of Christ"). [Matthew 4:1–11](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Matthew%204:1–11&version=nrsv), [Mark 1:12–13](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Mark%201:12–13&version=nrsv), and [Luke 4:1–13](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Luke%204:1–13&version=nrsv).[[156]](#cite_note-157) While not specifically instituted in the Bible text, the 40 days of fast and pray is also analogous to the 40 days during which [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses") repented and fasted in response to the making of the [Golden calf](/wiki/Golden_calf "Golden calf") (Exo. 34:27–28). (Jews today follow 40 days of repenting in preparation for and during the [High Holy Days](/wiki/High_Holy_Days "High Holy Days") from [Rosh Chodesh](/wiki/Rosh_Chodesh "Rosh Chodesh") [Elul](/wiki/Elul "Elul") to [Yom Kippur](/wiki/Yom_Kippur "Yom Kippur").)

## National No Smoking Day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: National No Smoking Day")]

In the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), Ash Wednesday is National No Smoking Day.[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159) The date was chosen because quitting smoking ties in with [giving up a luxury for Lent](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice"), and because of the link between [ash and smoking](/wiki/Cigar_ash "Cigar ash").[[159]](#cite_note-160)[[160]](#cite_note-161) In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [No Smoking Day](/wiki/No_Smoking_Day "No Smoking Day") was held for the first time on Ash Wednesday in 1984[[161]](#cite_note-162) but is now fixed as the second Wednesday in March.[[162]](#cite_note-163)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ash_Wednesday&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** Not all Catholics observe Ash Wednesday. [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), which do not count [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") as part of Lent, begin the penitential season on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"), the Monday before Ash Wednesday, and Latin Catholics who follow the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite") begin it on the First Sunday in Lent. Ashes are blessed and ceremonially distributed at the start of Lent in the Latin Church, the [Maronite Church](/wiki/Maronite_Church "Maronite Church"), and the [Syro-Malabar Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malabar Catholic Church"). In the Ambrosian Rite, this is done at the end of the Sunday Mass or on the following day.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)
